Alright, so now we get to the fun stuff. Now we get to the tactical, the scripts and stuff. So like I said, we're going to start down here at the bottom of the value ladder. And it wasn't going to go up like this, but we only have Robbie for a couple hours. And so Robbie stuff is awesome. And this is the high ticket stuff that a lot of you guys are wanting and needing. So we're going to go from here to here, and then we'll come back to the middle stuff. So, okay, so I'm going to start with the first two scripts. So the first one, I call this the Enquirer script. And do you guys like my image? I was really proud of this one. You've got the 300 pound baby. So I call this the Enquirer interrupts because this is like the shortest script in the world. This is like three or four words, right? But it's the initial script you need to get somebody to take action, okay? Especially when they're cold traffickers, someone that's not, that's new to you. You have to be able to communicate them in a very, very short period of time, right? We're down to our nine second thing, right? We've got very short period of time to catch them. And so back in the day, we used to write these headlines that were super big. And I think you can write bigger headlines when people are on more of the product wear cycle, right? We're talking about the different levels. But initially, when we're trying to grab someone's attention, we have to be short, fast, and punchy, okay? So if you look at probably the best example of that in the world, it's National Enquirer. Okay? I'm probably the only one in this room that does this today, but hopefully by tomorrow, all of you guys will do this. Whenever I'm trying to write headlines, I go to Google Images. I type in National Enquirer and all these National Enquirers pop up. And I look at headlines, okay? When we first launched our supplement, NURSEL, I had an 18 word headline. I remember showing it to one of my buddies named Aaron Lilly. And Aaron's got a product. I mean, you guys, when you fly on the airplane, you guys look at Sky Mall Magazine. You know what Sky Mall Magazine, there's that product that's in every single one that has this cream and you put it on your mole and your mole falls off. You guys seen that before? Okay? That's Aaron's product. He does insane numbers with that and he's killing it. So he's one of my friends that's just making the most money I know in the supplement space. And I showed him my headline, I showed him my whole site. And he's like, the biggest problem is like, your headline is like 18 words. He's like, in this market, you've got like this much time to capture the attention. He's like, you got to think National Enquirer. He's like, this needs to be three or four words max. Like, crap, how am I going to do that? And this is the way that I started coming over here and started looking at these. So I don't know how well you can see these that are in your book too, but like, when you look at the headlines, like, these are awesome. Hillary Clinton adopts alien baby. Sarah Palin's dark secrets. John and Kate's $10 million divorce. Angelina walks in on Brad and Jen. Jamie Lynn pregnant again. Al Gore sex attack. Kim Zafoni. Is JFK alive? Oprah, only three years to live. These are freaking good, huh? You think the best copywriters on planet earth work for the National Enquirer, you guys. It's one of the most highly red newspapers in the world. You guys realize that? I don't think there's any truth at all involved, but these headlines are amazing. And they're used when you're in the shopping, you know, the stand with your grocery, you're walking through, they would grab your attention and be like, whoa, what? You got to put it in, right? Okay, that's what's happening every day on the internet. There's all this stuff happening. People are shopping, they're buying all this stuff, and you have to be so big and so bold and so fast to grab their attention. Just like them walking in the aisle, they're like, whoa, what? You kidding me? They got to pull it in and bring it over, right? That's what we call these inquire interrupts. It's a pattern interrupt. We're taking people from the thousand emails they get a day, from the two million banner ads they see, from the 50,000 blogs they're surfing on, from Facebook reading, you know, a million different things and images and all this stuff that's happening, and you have to just boom, interrupt them. Okay, Shailoh was asking me yesterday to his, we're going to say, how come all the ads are ugly and they have red and yellow on them? Because you've got to grab these people and pull them away. She's like, why want nice ads that are browns and yellows? Now you can do that, okay? But that kind of stuff happens over here. That's when we shift from direct response, shock and awe, we got to grab these people in. After they're in front door, they're on your list, now we switch to like a branding messaging, right? When we're branding, you can do the nice stuff, you can do longer headlines, all these other things you can do. But initially, point of sale, we are grabbing somebody. We got to basically, they're walking by, we got to grab them by the shirt, yank them over here and say, boom, come by my crap, and then we start the process, right? And after they like you, know you trust you, attractive character stuff, now we can switch to nice ads, brands, all these other things. But initially, you got to think national inquire. That's how we suck people in initially, okay? Now you want to see how this relates to internet marketing? So if you look over in your book, I got three or four pages, these are some of the highest converting banner ads right now running on the internet. And you'll see how these all look like national inquire type headlines. Okay, so when I started looking for our banner ads for our NURSEL product, I was looking at all these, these are tons of ones that I found that are really, really good. So I'm going to read some of these to you. 70 year old grandma looks 40. Dr. Oz, better than a facelift. Learn how to naturally boost your free testosterone levels. Eat this, never diet again. FEMA ban, this is the number one item to hoard. Do you know, do you know, and skin tag remove, remove, removal creams work? Really work, yes. Make powerful healing water. You know, seven survival secrets. This device has grocery stores panicked. Texas man exposes fraud. You know, seven survival secrets. Gun gravers hate this. Man cheats credit score. One simple trick by credit score jump, 217 points. Banks hate this. You have these symptoms. You trust Obama. Three things that repel women. You see how good these are? Like you're on a news site reading, but that's the thing you see this banner ad, you're going to click on it, right? And that's funny. The people that are running the nice professional ones, look at the companies that are running and the companies like Allstate, like things like that, who have insane advertising budgets and they don't care if it works. They're just trying to be everywhere. Literally, one of my old employees got a job for this company down in Utah and he's an ad buyer for them. And this is his job. Every single day or every single month, a client will give him $10,000. The only thing they ask in return is they want to know back how many impressions did you get me for that much money. That's it. They don't ask how many clicks, how many sales, they don't even care. All they care about is impressions. So that's what we're competing against. People who just buy and crap, they don't care. They're getting paid just to get impressions. So literally he's like, all I do is I just burn their money because all they want, the only metric, the only KPI they have is how many impressions because then they're mind, they're like, they're branding, right? They just want to put it out there everywhere. Those ones do not make money. I would be shocked if you found a single company that has a nice beautifully designed banner that's working. Everybody I know who tracks their numbers to a T, this is what their banner ads look like. Okay? Yes. I'm talking about cold traffic. There's no idea who you are. You're trying to grab them. After you've got them in your funnel, after they've joined your list, whatever that is, you shift the message. Now it switches to branding for women, for us, for everything. I feel like our page is typically as ugly as they are. There's no brand, there's no nothing. We're trying to grab them, suck them in. After we've got them, now we shift to branding because now I want to take that relationship to the next level. That's when we introduce attractive character, our sites look nice, the way we sell is different. All the stuff is different because I agree. Because at that point, I'm not trying to grab them. I'm trying to build that relationship and I want a warmer, a warmer, better feel. That's kind of my, I have a lot of conversation with people about that. They're like, well, I like doing branding sites versus, I'd rather have a nice site that looks good versus one that converts really well because I want my people to love me. I'm like, no, you need both of them. You need this one on the front to grab them and then after you've got them, now you warm them up and change that relationship. That's my opinion, that's how we do ours and that's what I know works. Does that help hopefully? Yeah, so similar web, so when you go similar web and you have all the stuff, you scroll down, there'll be a section that says ads and it says show banner ads. You click on that and it'll show you all the ads that are running. And you get similar web, the free version only show you like four or five ads. You have to use like what runs where or if you use ad be or even the paid version similar web, it'll show you a whole bunch more. And so these are the ones that I pulled off that have been running for a long time. Consistently, all those kinds of things where these ones all came from. So after we did all this, we started looking back and like, what's going to be our headline for NERS? So we tried a whole bunch of different things. I think the winner right now, our winning banner ad, simply says weird nerve pain miracle. That's the headline. That's all it is. And then our image, we're always trying to find weird images because weird images is what catches you. On Facebook, the weird images ones you're like, what is that? A couple of things when we found like, if there's eyes looking at you increases conversion rate. If you notice back here with National Enquirer, all these images, there's always eyeballs. Something about eyeballs, we just, we get sucked to them, right? It's like having eyes. The other thing that's working really good on Facebook and on banner ads is inverting images. So for example, we had this weird random picture of blueberries that nothing to do with our product. We had it on there and then we did the negative version of it. This looks like, just looks weird. That's done awesome. Huge click through rate. It says weird nerve pain miracle and it's reverse. They're talking about reverse like the black and white back. Anyway, that shows that it just converges well. So half of it is these little quick headlines and half of these images and the weird of the images, the better. Like this dude, where the three things repel women. He got this guy showing his beard like, what is that? I don't know, but you see that and you're like, what is that? Right? The image doesn't necessarily have to be related as much. But it looks good. I mean, you feel like some of these images, I think these images are fantastic. Like this one right here, like breaking something, let's say breaking something worse. First, Obamacare, now there's something much worse planned. You got all these soldiers here in a video. I'm like, what the crap is about to happen, right? This one is really good. How celebrities get ripped in 2014? Big lady, skinny lady, you know, like the images are compelling. This one's awesome, dude. Seven survival secrets of these guys and gas masks running. Like, anyway, I think they're really good. So what I'd recommend doing is you guys, when you're on surf and on the internet, for me, whenever I'm on the internet, all I'm doing, like, maybe I'm kind of this advertising junky person. Like, for example, outside the internet, like when I drive and listen to radio, like, I hate the songs. I love when the commercials come on. That's my favorite part. Watch TV, my wife tries to fast forward. I'm like, no, this is why we're watching TV. This is the best part, right? And this is how the internet should be for you as well. Like, when you're surfing on blogs and stuff, like, you can go read your articles, but the number one goal of doing anything is trying to find the banner ads on the sites. I have a file where every time I see a couple of banner ads, copy and paste, and I save it, I've got folders and folders, just tons and tons of banner ads, because I never know which one's going to inspire me for my next thing and my next weird thing, right? And what's the next headline? And so whenever you're on any of your favorite sites, just start looking at the banner ads and grab them. That's, I mean, start building your swipe file, those kind of things. The pattern interrupt is happening here on the banner. Typically on the squeeze page, not always, but the majority of time, the squeeze page message is matching what's here on this banner ad. Here on the Facebook ad, wherever the ad is. So kind of like, if they see banner ads, like 300 pound baby, you see video, then they come over here and there's a thing saying, hey, 300 pound baby, give me your email address and I'll show you that video. Okay? So I'm matching that. They're kind of going the same way. Yeah. Round, very first test, I make the headline the exact same. And I might try to beat it, but for the most part, it's pretty similar. If that 300 pound baby was what got them to click, the next page would be about 300 pound baby, because that's what's going to get them to the next action. Just like email, when we have, when the subject line of the email says 300 pound baby, one thing we found that was interesting, this is actually really interesting. So, and you've probably, you've probably noticed me do this in a lot of my emails sometimes. I don't always do my emails, my internal list, but when we're sending out, when we're buying email ads, we always do this. So here's, here's the email, right? Here's like the subject line. Subject, what we found is you typically have your email right here, then in the middle, there's like the blue link and then there's like more email and there's blue link and then your email, right? And before we always be like, we just put the website URL there. So we're like, hey, I got this really cool thing. Check it out here. And then www.russleschool.com and then blah, blah, blah, blah, russleschool.com, right? We do that way a lot of times. Sometimes we do like, we have something and this link would say click here. We did that for a while. And what we tested a little while ago that beat everything is whatever the subject line was, so the subject line is weird nerve pain miracle, which is our control right now for all of our nurse email. This, the text on this link is weird nerve pain miracle. Okay, so it's congruency. And they saw that right now and it got them to open and they see that again and they get some to click. Cause I know that got it, it was good enough that it got them to open and I changed that to the text of the link is the exact same as the text of the subject line dramatically increased click rates. Okay, then we took the next step and we took that exact same thing and that became the headline of the landing page increased opt-in rates. Okay, cause we found out what was speaking to him. What was the most important thing? So this becomes the same as these becomes the same as this. Now in some situations you probably could beat it, but by default step one, I would just do it that way and, and try to beat it from there. But step one, I would just, I would lead with that. It's kind of cool, huh? All right. Any questions about the pattern interrupt stuff there? Okay, same pattern idea, same thing with our Facebook ads. Think about the Facebook ads you guys click on. Like anytime you click on an ad, especially in Facebook, like right before you click stop, take a little screen capture of it because if it caused you to want to click, that's a good sign it's probably a good ad. I always, every time I take an action, I'm like, wait, why did I do that? What caused, what gave me the emotional need for me to click on that thing? What was the, the reasoning? I love trying to figure out stuff out and I'm like, how can I copy that? How can I do the same thing in my ads? Okay. So that's, that's the very, very, very front of the value ladder script. I don't see anything, we're just creating ads trying to get people to come over and click, right?
